aliases.name: भूत (bhūta)
aliases.meaning: 'that which has been'; can mean spirit, element, or ghost

aliases.name: churel / chudail
aliases.meaning: Female bhoot of a woman who died in childbirth, with reversed feet

aliases.name: pret
aliases.meaning: Hungry ghost; specific class of bhoot

description.summary: A bhoot is the restless ghost of a deceased person in South Asian (Hindu, Muslim, Sikh, Bengali, Pakistani, Bangladeshi) folklore — typically someone who died violently, prematurely, or without proper funeral rites and so cannot pass to the afterlife.
description.full: Bhoot (Sanskrit bhūta, from a root meaning 'past' or 'that which has been') is the most widespread South Asian term for a restless ghost. In Hindu tradition, a bhoot is the spirit of a deceased person who has not progressed to the afterlife, typically because of a violent or untimely death, unfulfilled obligations, or improper funeral rites. Bhoots are said to be recognisable by certain distinctive signs: backward-facing feet, casting no shadow, speaking with a nasal voice, floating just above the ground (which is sacred), and a preference for white clothing and certain trees. Sub-categories include the churel (a woman who dies in childbirth), the pret (hungry ghost), and the pishacha (carnivorous flesh-eating spirit). Methods of warding off bhoots in folk practice include water, iron or steel objects, the smell of burnt turmeric, sprinkling earth on oneself, and invoking deities or holy figures. In Hindu practice, the appropriate response is not destruction but atma-shanti, ritual peace-giving derived from the Atharva Veda, intended to allow the spirit to be reborn or to complete unfinished business. Bhoot legends are deeply embedded in modern South Asian language, literature, cinema, and television — from the Bengali Thakurmar Jhuli stories to numerous Bollywood horror films.

etymology.origin: Sanskrit bhūta, past participle of the root √bhū ('to be, to become'), meaning 'that which has been' or 'past existence'.
etymology.meaning: 'The past one'; one who once was; a ghost or spirit. The same root underlies the elements (pañca-bhūta) in Hindu cosmology, but in folk usage 'bhoot' refers specifically to the restless dead.
etymology.first_use: Vedic Sanskrit (c. 1500–500 BCE); the term has continuous textual use across classical and medieval Sanskrit literature.

taxonomy.variant: South Asian (Hindu, Muslim, Sikh, Bengali, Punjabi)

related_phenomena.name: Churel / Chudail
related_phenomena.description: Female bhoot; spirit of a woman who died in childbirth, with reversed feet

related_phenomena.name: Vetala
related_phenomena.description: Vampiric corpse-possessing spirit (e.g. Vikram and Vetala stories)

related_phenomena.name: Djinn
related_phenomena.description: Islamic supernatural beings sometimes conflated with bhoot in South Asian Muslim tradition

related_phenomena.name: Rakshasa
related_phenomena.description: Hindu demonic class, distinct from but conceptually neighbouring bhoot

features.description: The classic giveaway: feet point backwards while the body faces forward
A bhoot casts no shadow, even in bright sunlight
Considered to avoid contact with the sacred earth; reported to hover slightly above the ground
Bhoots are said to speak with a distinctly nasal tone
Often described in white sari or white shroud
Specific trees (peepul, banyan, tamarind) are traditionally associated with bhoots, especially churels

physical.morphology: Generally human in form, often resembling the deceased person. Some bhoots can take animal forms; churels and pishachas are described in more monstrous detail.

abilities.description: Bhoots can change form, often appearing as familiar humans or as animals
Bhoots can possess the living, requiring atma-shanti or exorcism by tantriks or molvis

abilities.ability: Aversion to iron, water, and burnt turmeric
abilities.description: Folk wards include iron objects, splashing water, the smell of burnt turmeric, and invoking deities

habitat_preference: Sites of violent or untimely deaths
patterns: Most active at night and at dusk
Appear at sites associated with their death
Pursue unfinished business (vengeance, longing, attachment to family)
Most often encountered alone by a single witness

range.description: Pan-South Asian: India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and the South Asian diaspora (Caribbean, East Africa, UK, North America). Term and beliefs cross Hindu, Muslim, Sikh, and Christian South Asian communities.

first_recorded.source: Sanskrit bhūta in the Vedas and Upanishads

peak_activity_periods.description: Earliest textual references to bhūta as spirits and ghosts in Sanskrit literature
Extensive treatment in Puranic and Tantric literature
Rich folklore preservation through Bengali compilations (Thakurmar Jhuli, 1907) and modern South Asian cinema

origins.cultural_roots: Vedic Sanskrit cosmology and pan-South-Asian folk practice. The bhoot complex blends Hindu cremation theology, regional ghost beliefs, and (in Muslim communities) elements of Islamic djinn/jinnat tradition.
origins.earliest_accounts: Vedic and Upanishadic references to bhūta and pret as classes of spirits; ritual texts including the Atharva Veda.
origins.folklore_connections: Connected to broader South Asian supernatural traditions: pret, pishacha, vetala, churel, dakini, and (in Muslim contexts) djinn, jinnat, and shaitan.

timeline.event: Sanskrit terms bhūta and pret appear in Vedic literature, especially the Atharva Veda
Dakshinaranjan Mitra Majumder publishes Thakurmar Jhuli, a Bengali fairy-tale collection rich with bhoot stories
timeline.significance: Canonical folk-literature reference for Bengali bhoot lore

timeline.event: Bhoot as a recurrent figure in South Asian cinema (Mahal 1949; Bees Saal Baad 1962; Madhumati 1958)
Ram Gopal Varma's film 'Bhoot' marks a contemporary horror revival in Bollywood

history.modern_era: Bhoot beliefs remain culturally active across South Asia and the diaspora, both in domestic practice (haunted houses, ancestor rites) and in media (television series such as Aahat and Ssshhhh...Koi Hai, and a continuous stream of horror films). Academic study by ethnographers and anthropologists (e.g. Beth Roy, Diane Mines) has treated bhoot beliefs as a window onto rural South Asian moral order.

documentary.name: Atharva Veda — Bhoot ritual texts
documentary.description: Vedic ritual hymns and incantations addressing bhuta-prets, sources for the atma-shanti tradition.
documentary.analysis: Foundational Sanskrit textual basis for South Asian ghost theology.

documentary.name: Thakurmar Jhuli (1907)
documentary.description: Bengali compilation of folk tales by Dakshinaranjan Mitra Majumder containing many canonical bhoot stories (Mechho-bhoot, Shankchunni, Petni, Brahmadaitya).
documentary.analysis: The single most influential text for the modern Bengali popular image of bhoots.

paranormal.name: Restless souls awaiting moksha
paranormal.description: A bhoot is the soul of a person whose attachments, violent death, or improperly performed funerary rites prevent its progression to rebirth or final liberation; the appropriate intervention is atma-shanti rather than destruction.

paranormal.name: Djinn / spirit overlap (Muslim South Asia)
paranormal.description: In South Asian Muslim folk practice, bhoot beliefs overlap with Islamic djinn lore; the two categories are not always neatly separated in Indian, Pakistani, and Bangladeshi villages.
evidence_cited: Ethnographic accounts of taveez (amulet) and ruqyah practice for bhoot/djinn possession

scientific.name: Sleep paralysis and grief
scientific.description: Many bhoot encounters fit the cross-cultural pattern of sleep paralysis hallucinations and bereavement-related apparition experiences.
proponents: Cross-cultural sleep-paralysis researchers (Adler, Jalal et al.)
evidence_cited: Survey studies of South Asian sleep-paralysis prevalence

scientific.name: Social functions of ghost belief
scientific.description: Anthropologists treat bhoot beliefs as performing social work: enforcing kinship duty (correct funerary rites), policing the boundaries of caste and gender (especially in churel narratives about women's deaths), and articulating community history.
evidence_cited: Village ethnographies in Bengal and Tamil Nadu

skeptical.name: Folkloric and cultural construct
skeptical.description: Mainstream Indian rationalist groups (Andhashraddha Nirmoolan Samiti, FIRA) treat bhoot belief as a sometimes-harmful superstition, particularly when used as a cover for exploitative 'exorcisms' targeting women.
evidence_cited: Documented cases of harmful exorcism practices, including witch-hunt incidents in Jharkhand and Assam

traditions: Bengali bhoot folklore (Mechho-bhoot, Shankchunni, Petni, Brahmadaitya, Skondhokata)
South Asian Muslim folk practice mixing bhoot and djinn
folklore.beliefs: Bhoot beliefs are widespread and culturally normalised; many otherwise modern households maintain rituals around death anniversaries (shraddha) explicitly tied to preventing the deceased from becoming a bhoot.

indigenous.significance: Bhoot is woven into death-rite, kinship, and gender ethics across South Asia.
indigenous.sensitivity_note: Bhoot belief intersects with serious social harms (witch-hunts, sham exorcisms targeting widows). Documentation should distinguish folkloric tradition from contemporary abuse.
indigenous.respect_guidelines: Cite South Asian writers and ethnographers; do not flatten Hindu, Muslim, and regional variants into a single 'Indian ghost'.

literature: Lal Kella — Satyajit Ray (Feluda series, Bengali ghost themes)
The Sandalwood Tree — Bengali ghost story collections
other: 'Bhoot bangla' (haunted house) as a household term
South Asian horror as a genre across Hindi, Bengali, Tamil, Telugu, and Malayalam cinema

researchers.name: Sudhir Kakar
researchers.contribution: Psychoanalytic study of South Asian supernatural beliefs (Shamans, Mystics and Doctors, 1982)

researchers.name: Diane P. Mines
researchers.contribution: Ethnography of Tamil village ghost beliefs

organizations.name: Maharashtra Andhashraddha Nirmoolan Samiti (MANS)
organizations.focus: Rationalist organisation campaigning against harmful applications of ghost belief in India

research.ongoing_studies: Contemporary anthropological research engages bhoot belief through the lens of gendered violence, witch-hunts in eastern and central India, and transnational horror cinema.

sources.title: Shamans, Mystics and Doctors: A Psychological Inquiry into India and Its Healing Traditions
